visits.text: The Little Rann of Kutch spreads across 4,953 km²—a semi-arid salt desert sheltering the Indian Wild Ass Sanctuary. It's the only place on Earth where the Indian wild ass, the ghudkhar, survives—a swift animal capable of outrunning predators. The sanctuary also holds jackals, hyenas, and nilgai. Closed during monsoon (July–September) when the plain floods.

activities.text: A safari here looks nothing like the others: no dense vegetation, no hides. Just the white horizon of salt pans and silhouettes that emerge. Ghudkhars live in herds led by a stallion—you spot them by their dark dorsal stripes. Book a jeep safari from Dasada or Zainabad local resorts; morning departures (before 8am) are most productive between October and March.

Pink dawn over the salt marshes: flamingos take off by the hundreds from Nawa Talao lake near Dasada. The Little Rann hosts over 250 species of resident and migratory birds, including demoiselle cranes and steppe buzzards in winter. Bring binoculars—distances here deceive.

days_plan.itin_title: Gujarat: Grand UNESCO Sites Tour in 15 Days

visits.text: Dharamsala is about 5 hours by road from Amritsar - the road gradually climbs toward the Himalayan foothills. Your destination is McLeod Ganj, 'little Lhasa,' perched at 1,770m altitude. Since 1959, this is where the Dalai Lama has lived, after fleeing Chinese-occupied Tibet. Begin with the Tsuglagkhang complex, the spiritual heart of the Tibetan community in exile. The main temple houses a 3-meter golden statue of Buddha Shakyamuni, flanked by Avalokiteshvara and Padmasambhava. The atmosphere is peaceful - monks in crimson robes, prayer wheels spinning, whispers of mantras.

Right next door, the Kalachakra Temple contains frescoes depicting the 722 deities of the mandala, painted over three years by three master painters under the Dalai Lama's personal direction. Photography is forbidden, but the detail level of the mandalas is stunning.

Visit the Tibet Museum at the complex entrance. On two floors, it traces Tibet's history as an independent nation, the Chinese occupation since 1949, and exile. Testimonies from political prisoners and archival photos are deeply moving. Allow at least 45 minutes.

Walk down to Namgyal Monastery, the Dalai Lama's personal monastery, founded in 1564. If you're there around 1:30pm (except Sundays), you can watch the monks' debates in the courtyard - a fascinating spectacle where arguments are punctuated with theatrical hand-clapping. The monks are usually delighted to chat with visitors.

activities.text: Walk to Bhagsu Waterfall, about 2km from McLeod Ganj. The trail crosses Bhagsu village and climbs toward the waterfall through rocks. Bring walking shoes. At the top, several cafés with valley views serve momos (Tibetan dumplings) and butter tea.

Wander McLeod Ganj's lanes - Tibetan shops sell singing bowls, thangkas (fabric paintings), woolen shawls, and turquoise jewelry. Cafés serve authentic Tibetan cuisine: thukpa (noodle soup), steamed or fried momos, and the famous yak butter tea. A world apart from the Indian plains.
Take a thangka painting or Tibetan cooking class - several centers in McLeod Ganj offer half-day workshops. It's a concrete way to touch Tibetan culture beyond surface tourism.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Northern India Circuit: Delhi, Varanasi & the Himalayas

visits.text: Nothing to see here, you think upon arriving at this salt island. Then the excavations appear: streets laid out with precision, water reservoirs, a citadel. Dholavira is one of the largest known Harappan cities, occupied between 3000 and 1500 BCE. Listed by UNESCO in 2021, it stretches over 100 hectares on Khadir Bet island, in the Great Rann of Kutch. A signboard with ten Harappan letters discovered here is the oldest example of panel writing found at any Indus Valley civilization site.

Discovered during excavations launched in 1967-68, Dholavira's archaeological museum assembles pottery, copper tools, measuring instruments, and stone ornaments. It helps decode the site's seven occupation phases—from zenith to abandonment, then reoccupation with a noticeably impoverished culture. Open 9am–5pm, closed Friday.

activities.text: Visit Dholavira's archaeological museum before touring the main site—the context provided makes the ruins legible. Bring water (no shops on-site) and a hat: the sun pounds mercilessly on the white stones. The access road is long and isolated; prefer arrival in early afternoon to depart before dark.

days_plan.itin_title: Gujarat: Grand UNESCO Sites Tour in 15 Days

visits.text: Underfoot, the salt cracks. At the horizon, the white of the Great Rann of Kutch meets the white of the sky with no telling where one ends. Dhordo is the gateway village to the salt desert and the starting point for Rann Utsav, a cultural festival organized by Gujarat Tourism between November and February. Outside the festival, the desert is accessible via an entry permit (rannpermit.gujarat.gov.in). Sunset over the salt pans resembles nothing else.

activities.text: If the full moon coincides with your passage through the Great Rann, plan your night here: the white desert under a full moon is one of the strangest panoramas on the subcontinent. Rann Utsav offers musical and artisan evenings at Dhordo's official tent city, with Kutchi embroidery, bandhani, and mirror work. Reservation necessary in high season (December–February).

Head down to Dhordo village in the morning to meet the Rabari and Mutwa communities, pastoral nomads whose mirror embroidery stands out at first glance. The village hosted the first G20 tourism working group in 2023. Artisan women sell direct; prices are negotiated face-to-face.

days_plan.itin_title: Gujarat: Grand UNESCO Sites Tour in 15 Days

visits.text: Built between 1535 and 1541 by Dom Nuno da Cunha after the defeat of the Sultan of Gujarat, Diu Fort watches over the island's eastern tip from its double moat filled with sea. Its 20-meter walls, 22 cannons, and interior tunnels are in good condition. The Portuguese remained until Operation Vijay in 1961—426 years of presence visible in every stone.

St. Paul's Church defies categories: Portuguese Baroque planted on the Indian coast, built between 1601 and 1610, it details in carved wood angels, vines, and horsemen that owe more to Lisbon than Ahmedabad. Still active. Nearby, St. Thomas Church has become Diu Museum—colonial artifacts, marquetry, and nautical charts.

activities.text: Diu's lanes smell of dried fish and cold beer—rare in Gujarat where alcohol is prohibited. The island is a Union Territory, thus outside Gujarati prohibition. Nagoa Beach, the island's largest beach, is accessible by moto-taxi from town. The evening on Diu is quiet: no loud nightlife, just a few seaside bars.

days_plan.itin_title: Gujarat: Grand UNESCO Sites Tour in 15 Days

visits.text: Not many travelers stop at Eluru—wrongly. The Kolleru Bird Sanctuary covers 673 square kilometers between the Krishna and Godavari deltas and ranks as one of India's largest freshwater lakes. The site has been a Ramsar wetland since 2002. Between October and March, migratory birds arrive from Siberia: painted storks, glossy ibises, waterfowl. Walk-in access from the shores; boat available at Atapaka.

activities.text: You board a boat at sunrise on Kolleru: herons and cormorants take off at your approach, water lilies still closing their blooms in the calm water. The ride lasts about an hour from the fishing village of Atapaka. Rate negotiated directly with local fishermen.

days_plan.itin_title: India's East Coast: Chennai to Visakhapatnam, 13 Days

visits.text: Arrival at Ernakulam Junction after the night train. Take the local ferry (15 minutes) to Fort Kochi - it's the best way to arrive, with the silhouette of Chinese fishing nets appearing from the boat. These enormous cantilever nets, called 'Cheena Vala' in Malayalam, were allegedly introduced by Chinese merchants in the 14th century, possibly by explorer Zheng He's fleet. You won't find them anywhere else in India.

Step through the door of St. Francis Church, the oldest European church in India. Built in wood by Portuguese Franciscans in 1503, then rebuilt in stone in 1516, it housed Vasco da Gama's burial after his death in Kochi in 1524 - his remains were later repatriated to Lisbon in 1539. Portuguese tombstones are on the north wall, Dutch ones on the south. The church is open from 9am to 5pm (except Sunday morning).

Continue to Mattancherry for the Dutch Palace, built by the Portuguese around 1555 then renovated by the Dutch in 1663. Don't be fooled by the modest exterior: inside, the 17th-century murals illustrating the Ramayana are among Kerala's finest. Right next door, Jew Town and the Paradesi Synagogue (1568) - one of the Commonwealth's oldest active synagogues - are worth the detour for their hand-painted floor tiles imported from China.

activities.text: Fort Kochi is best explored on foot - the lanes are lined with Portuguese, Dutch and British colonial houses, interspersed with contemporary art galleries and cafes in former spice warehouses. If the Kochi Biennale (every 2 years, December to March) is on, art installations take over historic buildings - Aspinwall House, the old harbor warehouse, is the main site.

In the evening, attend a Kathakali performance (Kerala's dance-theater with spectacular makeup) at one of Fort Kochi's cultural centers - shows generally start around 6:30pm with a makeup demonstration 1 hour before. Otherwise, return to the Chinese nets at sunset: fishermen operate the wooden counterweights, and the low light on the nets creates Kerala's most photographed cliché.

days_plan.itin_title: South India Circuit: Hampi, Goa and the Nilgiri Hills

places.name: Goa

visits.text: Old Goa, 10 km from Panaji, is the historic heart of Portuguese colonisation — the 'Rome of the East' in the 16th century. The Basilica of Bom Jesus (1605), UNESCO-listed, houses the body of Saint Francis Xavier in a silver coffin. The Jesuit missionary died in 1552, but his body is said to be 'incorrupt' — it's publicly displayed every ten years (last time in 2024). The Baroque interior is restrained compared to the façades, but the gilded altarpiece is striking.

Just across the way, the Sé Cathedral is the largest church in Asia — 76 metres long, built between 1562 and 1640. It has only one tower (the second collapsed in 1776 and was never rebuilt). Inside, the Chapel of the Blessed Sacrament is lavished in gold. Allow 30 minutes for both churches together.

In the afternoon, head south to the beaches — Palolem is a crescent-shaped cove fringed with coconut palms, bookended by rocky headlands at each end. The water is calm, ideal for swimming. It's Goa's most photogenic beach, but also the busiest in peak season. If you're after more peace and quiet, continue to Agonda, 5 km further north — same scenery, fewer people.

Panaji (Panjim), Goa's capital, has a hidden Latin Quarter: Fontainhas. This tangle of narrow streets lined with houses painted yellow, blue and ochre is the last vestige of the old Portuguese district. The wrought-iron balconies, azulejos (decorative tiles) and corner chapels could easily teleport you to Lisbon. Allow an hour to wander with camera in hand.

Chapora Fort, made famous by Indian cinema, offers a 360° panorama over the coastline — Vagator beach below and the mouth of the Chapora River. The red laterite walls stand out sharply against the sky. Only ruins remain (the fort dates to 1717), but it's the viewpoint, not the architecture, that makes this place. Climb up in the late afternoon for the best light.

activities.text: Taste Goan cuisine — a unique blend of Indian and Portuguese influences. Vindaloo (pork marinated in vinegar and spices) and xacuti (chicken with coconut and spices) are the signature dishes. Pair them with feni, the local spirit distilled from cashew nuts or coconut palm sap.
Sunset on the beach — it's a ritual in Goa. Beach shacks serve grilled fish with your feet in the sand as the sun plunges into the Arabian Sea. A simple, perfect moment.

If you're here on a Friday, don't miss the Mapusa Market — the most authentic in Goa. Spices, chouriço sausages, tropical fruits, dried fish and local handicrafts. It's loud, colourful and thoroughly local.

In the evening, prepare for the Goa → Kochi leg: the Netravati Express (16345) departs Madgaon around 10:40 PM and arrives at Ernakulam Junction the next day around 12:20 PM — roughly 14 hours overnight. This is the famous Konkan Railway, one of the most beautiful train lines in the world: the track hugs the west coast, crossing 2,000 bridges and 92 tunnels between mountains and sea. Book an AC2 sleeper for a comfortable night. The Mangala Lakshadweep Express is another daily option. Reserve 4–5 days ahead on IRCTC.

visits.text: Locals still call it 'Golla Konda'—the shepherd's hill. Golconda Fort was transformed by the Qutb Shahis into an impregnable citadel with 10 km of ramparts and 87 bastions. The trick: clap your hands beneath the dome of Fateh Darwaza—the sound travels up to the summit nearly a kilometer away. The Koh-i-Noor passed through here before landing in London.

Seventy domed tombs rise from the gardens of Ibrahim Bagh, some gilded for sultans, others more austere for nobles. The Qutb Shahi Tombs were restored between 2013 and 2019 by the Aga Khan Trust. Entry requires effort: arrive on a weekday to appreciate the silence between the structures.

activities.text: Stay for the evening sound and light show at Golconda Fort: Amitabh Bachchan's voice narrates the 1687 siege by Aurangzeb. The performance lasts about 45 minutes. Book your ticket early—numbered seats sell out fast on weekends.

days_plan.itin_title: Deccan Trail: Bahmani Sultans and Kakatiya Temples in 12 Days

visits.text: Once a Kalinga trading port mentioned by Ptolemy under the name Paloura, Gopalpur has retained the melancholy of a forgotten town. Dilapidated colonial villas line quiet lanes, and the golden beach, bordered by casuarinas, is uncrowded. Known to Europeans since the colonial era, this resort served as a military port during World War I.

The white lighthouse stands at the far end of the beach, built in 1965. Open to visitors in the afternoon (variable hours), it offers 360-degree views over the Bay of Bengal and distant Chilika lagoon. Modest height but unobstructed view. Symbolic entry fee.

activities.text: Take your meals at the fishermen's shacks lining the beach: crabs, prawns, fish grilled that same morning. The cooking is simple and good. The Nolias, local fishermen, haul their nets by hand on the beach at dawn. Don't swim—currents are dangerous year-round.

days_plan.itin_title: From Coast to Jungle: Bhubaneswar to Bhopal in 16 Days

visits.text: Hampi has been a UNESCO site since 1986—the ruins of the former Vijayanagara Empire capital (14th-16th centuries) sprawl across 26 km² among enormous ochre granite boulders. Begin with Virupaksha Temple, the only one still active, dedicated to Shiva. Its 50-meter gopuram tower dominates the main bazaar. Inside, in a dark chamber, you'll witness an astonishing phenomenon: the inverted image of the gopuram projects naturally onto the wall through a camera obscura effect. Arrive early morning to beat the heat—it can be crushing after 11am.

Continue to Vittala Temple, 2.5 km northeast. This is Hampi's jewel: its stone chariot, drawn by carved elephants, appears on 50-rupee notes. The 56 musical pillars in the main hall produce different notes when struck—a 16th-century engineering feat. Take a tuk-tuk or rent a bicycle to connect the two temples; walking in full sun is exhausting.

activities.text: Late in the day, climb Matanga Hill for sunset. The ascent takes about 20 minutes via a rocky trail—wear good shoes and bring water. From the summit, the 360° view over the ruins, rice paddies, Tungabhadra River, and granite boulders is one of Karnataka's most beautiful. Descend before dark—the path isn't lit.

To explore the ruins further afield, rent a bicycle for the day and tour the royal enclosure: the Lotus Mahal (a pavilion in Indo-Islamic style), the elephant stables (eleven aligned domes), and the Queen's Bath. Allow 2 to 3 hours to cover this area by bike at a leisurely pace.

days_plan.itin_title: South India Circuit: Temples, Backwaters & Wellness

visits.text: Haridwar is only 25km from Rishikesh but the vibe is totally different - here, no hippies or trendy cafés. It's one of Hinduism's seven holiest cities, where the Ganges officially enters the plains. The city's heart is Har Ki Pauri, the most sacred ghat, where a footprint of Vishnu is said to be carved in stone. This is where millions of pilgrims come for ritual bathing. Every 12 years, Haridwar hosts the Kumbh Mela, the world's largest human gathering - over 100 million people in 2021.

Take the cable car (or walk up, 45 min) to Mansa Devi Temple, perched on a Shivalik ridge 300 meters above the city. The panoramic view sweeps over Haridwar, the Ganges, and the Gangetic plains stretching to the horizon. The temple is dedicated to goddess Mansa, who grants wishes - you'll see red threads tied by devotees.

On the opposite hill, Chandi Devi Temple (also cable-car accessible) allegedly dates from the 8th century and was founded by philosopher Adi Shankaracharya. The walk up (3km) crosses a forest with valley views - pleasant in the morning when it's cool.

activities.text: Attend Ganga Aarti at Har Ki Pauri at sunset - it's older and more 'authentic' than Rishikesh's, less packaged for tourists. Thousands of devotees crowd the ghats, temple bells ring, incense smoke mingles with river mist. Place a diya (floating lamp) on the Ganges and watch it drift in the current.

Wander through Bara Bazaar, Haridwar's main market. You'll find vessels for carrying Ganges water home (a sacred souvenir for Hindus), rudraksha (prayer beads), deity statues, and traditional sweets. The atmosphere is frenetic and colorful.

Sample Haridwar's strict vegetarian cuisine - the entire city is vegetarian (alcohol and meat are banned). Dhabas (eateries) around Har Ki Pauri serve complete thalis, kachori, and thick lassi. Haridwar's chole bhature is famous across North India.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Northern India Circuit: Delhi, Varanasi & the Himalayas

visits.text: Built in 1591 to mark the end of a devastating epidemic, Charminar was the first monument erected by Muhammad Quli Qutb Shah in his new capital of Hyderabad. The structure soars to 56 meters, flanked by four minarets framing a still-bustling crossroads. Climb to the second level for views over the bazaar: entry is free before 5 p.m., after which access becomes complicated.

Chowmahalla Palace deserves a full hour: this former seat of the Nizams was constructed in the 18th century, blending Mughal domes with Persian stuccowork. The Durbar Hall with its crystal chandelier leaves visitors speechless. Closed on Fridays; plan for ₹100 (foreign visitors: ₹400).

The call to prayer echoes through Mecca Masjid, one of the Deccan's largest mosques, whose construction spanned several decades under the Qutb Shahis and later the Mughals. The monolithic columns of the portico each weigh several tons. Enter early in the morning, before the Friday rush.

Touristy, perhaps, but Salar Jung Museum harbors improbable treasures: a 19th-century English automaton, Mughal miniatures, and a collection of jade daggers. The sprawling building on the Musi riverbank easily requires two hours. Arrive at opening to avoid school groups.

activities.text: Dive into the lanes of Laad Bazaar, famous for its lacquered bangles that have encircled the base of Charminar for centuries. The colors range from emerald to crimson; bargaining is expected, wandering is encouraged. Bring cash—small vendors don't take cards.

If you walk from Charminar at sunset, the golden light on the minarets creates an image photographs never quite capture. The stroll toward Purani Haveli takes 20 minutes. Wear closed shoes—the lanes are unevenly paved.

days_plan.itin_title: Deccan Trail: Bahmani Sultans and Kakatiya Temples in 12 Days

visits.text: See the Hawa Mahal (Palace of Winds) early when the sun strikes the facade—that's the magic hour. This 15-meter pink sandstone wall, built in 1799 by Maharaja Sawai Pratap Singh, contains 953 honeycomb windows. Architect Lal Chand Ustad designed it in the shape of Krishna's crown. The jharokhas allowed royal women to observe street processions without being seen. Surprising detail: the building has no foundation. Entry is from the rear, not the facade—search carefully.

Jaipur has been a trading city since its founding in 1727 by Maharaja Sawai Jai Singh II—one of India's first grid-plan cities. Johari Bazaar for gemstones, Tripolia Bazaar for textiles and lac bangles, Bapu Bazaar for souvenirs. Stop for roadside chai—often offered by shopkeepers hoping you'll linger.

The City Palace of Jaipur is still partially inhabited by the royal family. The Mubarak Mahal (Palace of Welcome), built in the late 19th century, houses a royal textile museum. Don't miss the two giant silver urns in the Diwan-i-Khas—standing 1.60 m tall, they're the world's largest solid silver objects (Guinness record). They carried Ganges water on royal trips to England.

Right next to the City Palace, Jantar Mantar is an astronomical observatory built in 1734 by Sawai Jai Singh II. Its 19 monumental stone instruments are a UNESCO site since 2010. The most imposing: the Samrat Yantra, the world's largest sundial—27 meters tall, accurate to 2 seconds. Hire a guide on-site, or the instruments remain incomprehensible.

If you have energy late in the day, head to Galtaji (Monkey Temple) 10 km east. Nestled in a rocky gorge, this 18th-century temple complex is overrun by macaques. Natural pools fed by springs are sacred. The main temple dedicated to Hanuman is decorated with murals. Guard your belongings from the monkeys—they're quick and opportunistic.

Start with Nahargarh Fort at dawn—the view over Jaipur from the ramparts is the city's finest. Built in 1734 by Sawai Jai Singh II then expanded in 1868, the fort dominates the Aravalli ridge. Inside lies Madhavendra Bhawan, with its symmetrical suites built for the maharaja's nine queens. If you're athletic, cycle up from the city—the road is steep but spectacular.

Descending, stop at Jal Mahal (Water Palace) for the photo. This 18th-century palace seems to float in the middle of Man Sagar Lake—in reality, four of its five floors are submerged. You can't enter, but the view from the shore, especially with the Aravalli backdrop, is one of Rajasthan's classic postcards.

The royal cenotaphs of Gaitore, nestled in a valley between Nahargarh and the city, are often ignored by tourists. These are the tombs of Jaipur's maharajas—the most elaborate, in carved white marble, are those of Sawai Jai Singh II and Sawai Pratap Singh. The place is peaceful and photogenic, especially in the morning.

Amber Fort is the most spectacular monument in the Jaipur region. Built from 1592 by Raja Man Singh I on a hill overlooking Lake Maota, it blends Rajput and Mughal architecture. The Sheesh Mahal (Mirror Palace) inside is dazzling—thousands of convex mirror fragments line walls and ceilings. The Diwan-i-Aam courtyard and the Ganesh Pol (with its colored tile mosaic) are unmissable. The fort is part of the six Rajasthani forts inscribed as UNESCO in 2013. Allow at least 2 hours.

Just below Amber Fort, don't miss Panna Meena ka Kund—a 16th-century geometric stepwell whose zigzagging staircases create a hypnotic pattern. Highly photogenic, and far less crowded than Abhaneri's Chand Baori.

If Indian textiles interest you, head to the Anokhi Museum in Amber, housed in a restored haveli. It traces the history of block printing (hand-stamping on fabric), an ancestral Rajasthani technique. Artisan demonstrations are often offered.

activities.text: Take a guided bicycle tour of Jaipur at dawn, before the heat and traffic. Several operators offer 2-3 hour circuits through the old city, with stops at the flower and vegetable markets, and breakfast with a local family.
Late in the day, settle on the ramparts of Amber for sunset. The golden light on the Aravalli hills and Lake Maota below composes a sumptuous tableau. Bring water—there are no vendors at the top.

visits.text: Jaisalmer Fort is unique in the world: it's one of the last fortresses still inhabited, home to around 3,000 people living within its golden sandstone walls. Founded in 1156 by Rawal Jaisal, it rises 76 metres above the Thar Desert on Trikuta Hill. The golden stone has earned it the nickname 'Sonar Quila' (Golden Fort) — at sunset, the ramparts seem to melt into the sand. Inside, visit the 12th-century Jain temples, particularly the Chandraprabhu Temple with its mind-boggling stone carvings — every square centimetre of stone is intricately chiselled.

Head down to the lower town to explore the havelis — grand old mansions of fabulously wealthy merchants, carved from the same golden sandstone as the fort. Start with Patwon Ki Haveli, the largest — five adjoining buildings constructed between 1805 and 1862 by a family that traded in brocade and opium. Every balcony, every window is a lacework of stone. Then move on to Nathmal Ki Haveli, built by two brothers who each worked on one side — spot the subtle differences between the two halves; it's a fun game.

Gadisar Lake, a 10-minute walk from the fort, is an artificial reservoir dug in 1367 by Maharawal Gadsi Singh. The entrance is through a carved gateway. Early in the morning, the fort's reflection in the still water is stunning. You can rent a pedal boat or simply sit on the ghats.

activities.text: Take an excursion into the Thar Desert to the Sam Sand Dunes, 42 km from Jaisalmer. Operators offer late-afternoon outings to catch the sunset over the dunes — by camel or by jeep. The sight of golden dunes bathed in raking light is breathtaking. If you spend the night under a tent, the Thar's starry sky, free from light pollution, is one of the most spectacular in India. You can also return the same evening if you'd rather sleep in town.

days_plan.itin_title: India Grand Tour: From the Taj Mahal to the Backwaters
Intensive Rajasthan Road Trip: Forts, Tigers & Taj Mahal

places.name: Jharasangam

visits.text: A detour worth taking: Sri Ketaki Sangameswara Swamy Temple in the village of Jharasangam is the only known exception to the Hindu rule forbidding ketaki flowers as offerings to Shiva. According to local legend, King Kupendra was cured of a skin disease after bathing in the temple's spring. Open from 5:30 a.m. to 9 p.m.; no accommodation on-site, continue to Bidar.

days_plan.itin_title: Deccan Trail: Bahmani Sultans and Kakatiya Temples in 12 Days

map_keywords: Sri Ketaki Sangameswara Swamy Temple Jharasangam

places.name: Jodhpur

visits.text: Mehrangarh dominates Jodhpur from a 125-meter rocky spur—it's one of India's most massive forts. Built from 1459 by Rao Jodha, it houses a remarkable museum with an excellent audio guide available in French. The collections of palanquins, armor and royal cradles are exceptional. Pass through Loha Pol gate—you'll see the handprints of queens who committed sati (immolation on their husband's funeral pyre). Allow 2 to 3 hours for the complete visit.

From Mehrangarh's ramparts, you'll understand why Jodhpur is the 'Blue City'—the indigo blue of the Brahmin quarter's houses stretches endlessly below. Descend into the lanes to lose yourself in this labyrinth of bright blue facades, carved doors and nonchalant cows. The best viewpoints are from the guesthouse terraces in the Navchokiya quarter.

Jaswant Thada, a 5-minute walk from Mehrangarh, is a white marble cenotaph built in 1899 in memory of Maharaja Jaswant Singh II. The marble is so thin it's translucent in sunlight. The garden and small artificial lake surrounding it make this a haven of peace after the fort's intensity.

Below the Clock Tower, Sardar Market is Jodhpur's beating heart—spices (Rajasthani red chili, turmeric), lac bangles, tie-dye textiles and miniatures. If you're seeking the city's best lassi, follow the locals—the most popular stalls are always the most crowded.

Push on to Toorji Ka Jhalra, an 18th-century stepwell recently restored, hidden among the old city houses. Less monumental than Bundi's, but the turquoise water and geometric stairs make it an unexpected photo spot.

Mandore Gardens, 9 km north of Jodhpur, shelter the cenotaphs of the Marwar rulers in a shaded park. The dewals (funerary temples) in red sandstone are carved with Hindu figures. The place is frequented by local families in the morning—a good glimpse of daily life away from tourist circuits. Watch for langurs, which are numerous and bold.

Rao Jodha Desert Rock Park, at the foot of Mehrangarh, is a 72-hectare ecological reserve created to restore desert vegetation. Marked trails traverse a landscape of volcanic rocks and endemic plants—euphorbias, commiphoras, acacias. If you enjoy walking, it's the perfect complement to the fort visit, with different views of the ramparts.

Umaid Bhawan Palace, part luxury hotel and part royal family residence, merits at least an outside glance. Built between 1929 and 1943, it's one of the last great palaces constructed in India—347 rooms, in sandstone and marble. The interior museum displays Art Deco collections and palace models.

activities.text: Operators offer zipline descents above Mehrangarh's ramparts and lakes—6 lines spanning 1.2 km with plunging views over the blue city and fort. Thrills guaranteed. Book the day before.

Set out on an excursion to the Bishnoi villages, 30 km from Jodhpur. The Bishnoi are a community renowned for their nature protection since the 15th century—they live in harmony with local wildlife (antelopes, gazelles, demoiselle cranes). Several operators offer half-day circuits that also include visits to potter and weaver artisans.

map_keywords: Rao Jodha Desert Rock Park Jodhpur

visits.text: Uparkot Fort is said to have been founded in 319 BCE by Chandragupta's Mauryan Empire—and archaeologists don't really contradict the date. The ramparts can reach 20 meters high in places. Sixteen sieges failed to take it; one reportedly lasted twelve years according to local tradition. Inside: two wells cut into living rock, a Buddhist cave complex, and an Ottoman cannon brought from Diu.

From Uparkot's ramparts, Girnar Hills dominate the eastern horizon—this volcanic massif shelters Jain and Hindu temples as well as the famous engraved edicts of Ashoka, among the oldest texts in Brahmi script. Girnar has a ropeway for travelers who prefer not to climb the steps.

Head up to Mahabat Maqbara, a 19th-century mausoleum dedicated to Bahaduddinbhai Hasainbhai, Nawab of Junagadh. Its facade fuses neo-Gothic pillars, Mughal balconies, and silver arabesques—a monument of assumed architectural oddity. Freely visited from the street.

activities.text: If your fitness allows, the roughly 10,000 steps leading to Girnar's summit temples are traditionally climbed at night to arrive at dawn. Pilgrims depart after midnight. For a more accessible version, the ropeway (Girnar Ropeway) serves the intermediate temples in minutes.

days_plan.itin_title: Gujarat: Grand UNESCO Sites Tour in 15 Days

visits.text: India's second-largest mangrove forest at 235 square kilometers, Coringa Wildlife Sanctuary sprawls across the Godavari estuary about twenty kilometers from Kakinada. More than 120 bird species are recorded here, plus saltwater crocodiles and the rare fishing cat. Open 9 a.m. to 5 p.m., closed Tuesdays. Modest entry fee.

River and sea merge into a blurred horizon at the Godavari mouth, visible from Coringa sanctuary. This estuary landscape—brown river waters meeting the blue-gray Bay of Bengal—looks like nothing else on the east coast. Best light late afternoon.

If weather permits, rent a boat at Kakinada port to reach Hope Island, an hour's crossing in the bay. This barrier island is a nesting site for Olive Ridley turtles and a bird refuge. No lodging on-site; return is in the afternoon. Verify boat service is operational before leaving—it's sometimes suspended.

Uppada Beach doesn't look like much from the road, but the traditional fishing nets stretched between wooden poles make it one of the delta coast's signature sights. Fishermen leave before dawn; by 7 a.m., the nets are already retrieved. The beach remains uncrowded by tourists.

activities.text: You walk the 600-meter wooden boardwalk snaking through the mangrove canopy: the plunging roots of Rhizophora frame the path, kingfishers cross the green shade. The boardwalk leads to a viewpoint over the estuary. Bring sturdy shoes; the wood can be slippery.

days_plan.itin_title: India's East Coast: Chennai to Visakhapatnam, 13 Days
India's East Coast: Chennai to Visakhapatnam, 13 Days

visits.text: Gulbarga Fort sees fewer visitors than Bidar, which is its main virtue. Originally built by a Kakatiya feudatory, it was fortified by the Bahmani in a half-Persian, half-Indian style. Inside, Jama Masjid oddly resembles Córdoba's Great Mosque: dozens of low domes on columns, no dominant minaret. Free entry.

Khwaja Bande Nawaz lived here for 22 years until the age of 105, spreading Sufism across the Deccan at Sultan Firuz's invitation. His dargah draws pilgrims from three states. The architecture blends Bahmani arches with Turco-Iranian paintings. Remove shoes at the entrance and cover your head.

activities.text: Detour to Haft Gumbaz, seven Bahmani dynastic tombs outside town. The complex is semi-abandoned, giving it an atmosphere over-restored sites have lost. Expect a 30-minute walk through a grassy field. No admission fee.

days_plan.itin_title: Deccan Trail: Bahmani Sultans and Kakatiya Temples in 12 Days

visits.text: Enjoy Varkala in the morning—descend the steps to Papanasam Beach (the "beach that washes away sins") for a morning swim. The water is warm year-round and the waves are moderate. Freshwater springs gush from the cliff directly onto the beach—a rare phenomenon. Head back up the cliff stairway, stopping at the little perched cafés along the way.

Take a ferry from Kanyakumari to reach Vivekananda Rock, 500 meters offshore. It was on this rock that the Hindu monk Swami Vivekananda meditated in 1892 before delivering his famous speech at the Parliament of Religions in Chicago. The memorial, built in 1970, blends architectural styles from across India. Right next to it, the statue of Thiruvalluvar—a 2nd-century Tamil poet—rises 40 meters above the ocean. Ferries depart every 30 minutes and the crossing takes 10 minutes. Avoid weekends and public holidays—the queue can last 2 hours.

activities.text: Kanyakumari is the southernmost point of India, where the Indian Ocean, Arabian Sea, and Bay of Bengal meet. Sunset from the Sunset View Point is the highlight: you'll see the sun plunge into the sea at the exact spot where three waters converge. Arrive 30 minutes early to secure a good spot—all of Kanyakumari gathers here.

End your circuit with a stroll along the Kanyakumari seafront. The seashell stands, chai stalls, and sea breeze offer a peaceful conclusion to your journey. The nearest airport is Trivandrum (Thiruvananthapuram), 90 km to the north—allow 2 hours' drive to reach it.

days_plan.itin_title: Tamil Nadu and Kerala Road Trip: Temples and Backwaters

visits.text: The Chettinad region, 90 km south of Trichy, is one of Tamil Nadu's best-kept secrets. In the 75 villages around Karaikudi, more than 10,000 palatial mansions were built by the Nattukottai Chettiars, a merchant community that made fortunes in maritime trade with Southeast Asia during the 19th and 20th centuries. The mansions blend Italian marble columns, Burmese teak, European stained glass, and handmade Athangudi tiles—a combination unique in the world. Many are closed, but a few like the VVR Palace welcome visitors for a modest fee.

The Kanadukathan Palace, 10 km from Karaikudi, is the most spectacular of the Chettinad mansions. Built in 1912 by Dr. Annamalai Chettiyar (founder of Indian Bank and Annamalai University), it contains a hundred rooms adorned with Spanish marble columns and crystal chandeliers. Visiting requires advance permission—ask your accommodation to arrange this. A few of these mansions have been converted into heritage hotels—sleeping in one is an experience in itself.

activities.text: Chettinad cuisine is renowned throughout India for its powerful spices—black pepper, star anise, fennel, and dried chili. If your accommodation offers it, don't miss a traditional meal served on a banana leaf. The Chettinad Chicken you may know from restaurants will be on a completely different level here—warning, it's seriously spicy.

Explore the villages on foot—every street corner reveals a new palace, a colorful temple, or a sacred tank. The Ayyanar temples, village guardians, with their giant terracotta equestrian statues, are typical of this region. Drive slowly between hamlets and let yourself be surprised by the scale of these mansions in the middle of the Tamil countryside.

days_plan.itin_title: Tamil Nadu and Kerala Road Trip: Temples and Backwaters

visits.text: 182 meters: the Statue of Unity looms before the car even reaches Kevadia. Inaugurated on October 31, 2018, the world's tallest statue is dedicated to Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel, India's first Deputy Prime Minister who unified over 565 princely states. The height of 182 meters corresponds exactly to the number of Gujarat Legislative Assembly seats. The observation gallery, at 135 meters, accommodates up to 200 simultaneous visitors overlooking the Narmada.

activities.text: Ascend to the observation gallery at 135 meters to look out over the Narmada River and Sardar Sarovar Dam framed by the Satpura and Vindhyachal hills. The complex also includes the Valley of Flowers, a museum, and a river cruise. Book tickets online at soutickets.in—queues can be long on weekends.

days_plan.itin_title: Gujarat: Grand UNESCO Sites Tour in 15 Days

visits.text: Khajuraho is about 4 hours from Orchha. UNESCO-listed since 1986, it's one of India's most extraordinary sites. Of the 85 temples built by the Chandela dynasty between 950 and 1050 AD, 25 have survived - and their sculptures are stunningly refined. Start with the Western Group, the most spectacular. Kandariya Mahadeva temple is the largest and most elaborate: its walls are covered with nearly 900 sculptures depicting deities, celestial nymphs (apsaras), and the famous entwined couples (mithuna). Contrary to belief, erotic scenes represent only about 10% of sculptures - the rest shows daily life, war, music, and dance.

Lakshmana temple, dedicated to Vishnu, is one of the best preserved. Built under King Yashovarman around 950, it rests on a high platform adorned with a continuous frieze of sculptures - elephants, horses, battle scenes, and court life. Take time to walk completely around the temple to appreciate the sculpture progression, from the earthly world at the base toward the divine at the summit.

Don't neglect the Eastern Group, 20 minutes' walk away. The Jain temples of Parsvanath and Adinath are less famous but equally finely carved, with floral motifs and apsaras of remarkable grace. The contrast between Hindu and Jain temples side by side testifies to the Chandela era's religious tolerance.

activities.text: Attend the sound and light show within the Western Group enclosure - it recounts Chandela history and temple construction. The show lasts about 50 minutes and takes place after sunset. The dramatic nighttime lighting of the temples alone is worth the trip.
Explore Khajuraho's small Archaeological Museum, which displays sculptures recovered from destroyed temple sites. Some pieces match the quality of those still in place on the temples.

Walk through Khajuraho village late afternoon - it remains surprisingly rural and quiet despite tourism. Local markets sell stone and wood carvings that imitate (with varying success) the temple style.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Northern India Circuit: Delhi, Varanasi & the Himalayas

visits.text: Khammam is a transit stop on NH163, midway between Bhadrachalam and Mancherial. Khammam Fort, built on a rock overlooking the town, dates from the Kakatiya period. Brief stop for dinner and overnight—the town has good accommodation options.

days_plan.itin_title: From Coast to Jungle: Bhubaneswar to Bhopal in 16 Days

visits.text: Two unremarkable hills from the road, yet carved in the 2nd century BCE with 33 Jain caves—18 on Udayagiri and 15 on Khandagiri. Commissioned by King Kharavela of the Mahameghavahana dynasty, these shelters housed monks during monsoon retreat. The Rani Gumpha cave is two stories high, decorated with scenes of royal processions. Allow 1.5 hours for both hills. Closed shoes essential.

You climb Udayagiri to reach Hathi Gumpha, where Kharavela's inscription carved in Prakrit details his conquests—one of ancient India's most important epigraphic texts. At the top of Khandagiri, an 18th-century Jain temple offers 360-degree views over Bhubaneswar. Sunrise recommended.

activities.text: The city of Bhubaneswar sprawls beneath your feet from the summit of Khandagiri: minarets, temple domes, green patches of parks. This morning panorama is free and largely ignored by organized tours. Bring water; the climb is short but exposed.

days_plan.itin_title: From Coast to Jungle: Bhubaneswar to Bhopal in 16 Days

places.name: Kochi

visits.text: You land at Cochin International Airport in Nedumbassery, roughly 40 km from Fort Kochi. Plan on a solid hour's drive to reach the historic quarter—the route cuts through paddy fields and coconut-lined canals that set the tone for the entire journey. Check into your accommodation and take time to acclimatize to Kerala's humid warmth.

Late afternoon, head to Fort Kochi Beach promenade to witness the Chinese fishing nets—massive cantilevered structures of teak and bamboo mounted on granite counterweights, in use since the 14th century and introduced, according to tradition, by merchants from Kublai Khan's court. The spectacle peaks at sunset, when fishermen work the nets by sheer muscle power. If hunger strikes, vendors right beside the nets will grill the fresh fish you pick from their catch.

Start at St. Francis Church, built of timber in 1503 by Portuguese Franciscan friars—it's the oldest European church in India. The current stone building dates to 1516. Vasco da Gama was buried here in 1524 during his third voyage to India; his remains weren't repatriated to Lisbon until 14 years later, but you'll still see his original tombstone inside. The church passed from Portuguese hands to the Dutch in 1663, then to the British in 1795—each occupant left its mark. Free entry, open 9am–5pm except during Sunday services.

A few minutes' walk brings you to Santa Cruz Cathedral Basilica, originally erected by the Portuguese in 1505 and rebuilt in its present neo-Gothic form in 1902. The interior will surprise you—pastel ceiling frescoes and a richly carved altar offer a burst of color that contrasts sharply with the whitewashed exterior. Stained-glass windows filter soft light into the nave, lending it a contemplative atmosphere even at midday.

Catch an auto-rickshaw or ferry to Mattancherry to visit Paradesi Synagogue, built in 1568 by Sephardic Jews who fled Spain and Portugal. The floor is paved with hundreds of hand-painted porcelain tiles imported from Canton in the 18th century—each one unique. 19th-century Belgian crystal chandeliers hang above a brass pulpit. The synagogue also preserves copper plates engraved in ancient Malayalam 1,600 years ago, detailing privileges granted to the Jewish community by the Cochin raja. Note: closed on Fridays, Saturdays, and Jewish holidays. Enter barefoot.

Exiting the synagogue, lose yourself in the alleys of Jew Town and Mattancherry spice market. The air is thick with cardamom, pepper, turmeric, and cloves—the same spices that drew Phoenicians, Romans, Arabs, and Europeans to this coast for millennia. Antique shops overflow with colonial furniture, vintage porcelain, and old coins. Bargain, but keep in mind that prices here are already more reasonable than Delhi or Mumbai.

The drive from Varkala to Kochi (184 km, 3.5 hours) takes you back north. If you leave early, you'll have time to visit Mattancherry Palace (also called the Dutch Palace), built by the Portuguese in 1555 as a gift to the Cochin raja, then renovated by the Dutch in 1663—hence its nickname. Don't miss the 17th-century murals illustrating scenes from the Ramayana and Mahabharata—they rank among Kerala's finest wall paintings. The palace also houses royal portraits, palanquins, and ceremonial garments.

If your flight is in the evening, use the remaining time to explore Jew Town's antique shops—vintage ceramics, textiles, and brass objects. Or enjoy one last Keralan meal at a Fort Kochi restaurant: a full thali with rice boat (rice shaped like a boat), fried fish, mango pickle, and payasam for dessert. Cochin International Airport is about 1 hour 15 minutes from Fort Kochi—allow a comfortable margin.

activities.text: Wander through Fort Kochi's lanes as dusk falls. Portuguese, Dutch, and British colonial facades succeed one another within a few hundred meters—an architectural palimpsest found nowhere else in India. You'll stumble upon contemporary art galleries installed in former spice warehouses, cafés tucked into Dutch houses, and street art murals. The atmosphere is serene, a world apart from the chaos of India's megacities.

In the evening, attend a Kathakali dance performance. This four-century-old theatrical form from Kerala blends dance, mime, and music. Artists spend over an hour applying makeup—green faces for heroes, red for villains—using natural pigments layered on thickly. Arrive 30 minutes before the show to watch the makeup application, often open to the public. Count on around 1.5 hours for the full experience.

Before leaving Kerala, if time allows, swing by Broadway Market in Ernakulam (the mainland part of Kochi). This covered market, far less touristy than Mattancherry, is where locals buy their spices, fabrics, and kitchen utensils. You'll find black pepper, green cardamom, and turmeric powder at unbeatable prices—perfect for useful souvenirs.

map_keywords: Santa Cruz Cathedral Basilica Fort Kochi

visits.text: Leave Alleppey southward for Kollam (70 km, 2 hours), one of the world's oldest trading ports. Marco Polo described it in the 13th century under the name "Coilum," and Ibn Battuta in the 14th century counted it among the five greatest ports he'd seen in 24 years of travel. Phoenicians, Romans, Chinese, and Arabs traded spices, silk, and porcelain here for millennia. Today, the town is the global cashew capital—you'll catch the roasting aroma in certain neighborhoods.

Visit Ashtamudi Lake, Kerala's second-largest body of water, a Ramsar-designated wetland. Its name means "eight arms" in Malayalam, referring to the eight channels branching through the mangroves. It's the southern gateway to the backwaters, far quieter than Alleppey. If you haven't had your fill of canals, a short cruise on Ashtamudi will offer wilder scenery, with coconut palms leaning over the water and sleepy fishing villages.

Detour to Munroe Island (Munroethuruthu), an archipelago of eight small islands at the confluence of Ashtamudi and the Kallada River. You reach it by boat. Life here has remained virtually unchanged—copra drying, toddy (palm wine) production, shrimp fishing in the canals. Allow 2 to 3 hours for a boat tour with a local guide.

activities.text: Late in the day, climb Thangassery Lighthouse, a relic of Portuguese and then Dutch presence on this coast. From the top, the view sweeps across the Arabian Sea and the red roofs of the old colonial quarter. Thangassery was the first European trading post established in Kollam in 1502. Catch the sunset from the adjacent beach.

days_plan.itin_title: Kerala Backwaters, Tea Hills & Coast Circuit

visits.text: Ten kilometers before arrival, the silhouette of Konark Sun Temple already cuts the sky. Built around 1250 by Narasimhadeva I of the Ganga dynasty, this monumental stone chariot represents the vehicle of the sun god Surya: 24 sculpted wheels, seven rearing horses. UNESCO since 1984. The main tower collapsed in the 16th century; what remains is already dizzying. Hire an accredited guide at the entrance—erotic and astronomical details require explanation.

At the Konark Archaeological Museum, just opposite the temple, you'll find sculptures recovered during excavations: horsemen, dancers, nagarajas. Less crowded than the temple, this is often where the finest pieces reside. Closed Fridays. Allow 40 minutes.

activities.text: Chandrabhaga Beach, 3 km from the temple, hosts the Magha Saptami festival each February when thousands of pilgrims come to bathe at sunrise. Outside the festival, it's an uncrowded, clean beach with colorful fishing nets. Swimming not recommended—strong currents. Excellent for photographing dawn.

days_plan.itin_title: From Coast to Jungle: Bhubaneswar to Bhopal in 16 Days

visits.text: Leave Mumbai via the Mumbai-Pune Expressway (115 km, 2.5 hours) heading to Lonavala, a hill station perched in the Western Ghats. The road snakes through tunnels and verdant valleys—during monsoon, waterfalls gush everywhere, but in the dry season the landscape is more arid.

Your first stop: Karla Caves, which house India's largest rock-cut Buddhist chaitya (prayer hall). Built between the 2nd century BC and 2nd century AD, this hall measures 45 meters long and 14 meters high—an underground cathedral carved from basalt. What strikes you first are the teak wood ribs on the ceiling: most are original, still in place after more than 2,000 years. The 37 octagonal pillars are topped with sculptures of couples riding elephants—a liveliness and naturalism that makes you forget their age. Count on 350 steps to climb from the base of the hill.

Eight kilometers from Karla, Bhaja Caves are a complex of 22 Buddhist caves from the 2nd century BC, contemporary with Karla but far less crowded. You'll find stupas, remarkable narrative sculptures, and an atmosphere of contemplative solitude that Karla has lost to tourism. Access requires about 200 steps on a rocky path—the views across the valley from the top are worth the effort.

activities.text: Late in the day, enjoy Lonavala—the town itself isn't exceptional, but the fresh Ghats air and views from viewpoints like Tiger's Leap or Rajmachi Point are welcome after Mumbai's heat. Taste chikki, the local specialty—a crunchy treat made from jaggery (unrefined cane sugar) and peanuts, sold everywhere in town.

days_plan.itin_title: Mumbai & Maharashtra Circuit: Sacred Caves and the Konkan Coast

visits.text: The Meenakshi Amman Temple is arguably the most spectacular temple in all of South India — and the one monument that justifies the detour to Madurai on its own. Its 14 gopurams (gateway towers) soar up to 52 metres high and are covered in thousands of brightly painted sculptures — gods, goddesses, demons, mythical creatures. The current temple dates from the 17th century under the Nayak dynasty, but a sanctuary has stood here for at least 2,000 years. Inside, 33,000 sculptures adorn the corridors. The sacred Golden Lotus Tank (Potramarai Kulam) sits at the heart of the complex. Visit early in the morning (opens at 5 AM) or in the evening — the ceremony of putting Shiva and Meenakshi to bed around 9 PM is an intense experience. Note: no shoes or shorts, and non-Hindus do not have access to the inner sanctum.

After the temple, pop over to the Thirumalai Nayakkar Mahal, a 17th-century palace built by King Thirumalai Nayak in 1636. Only a quarter of the original building survives (the king's grandson dismantled the rest to build a new palace in Trichy), but step into the main hall: its massive 12-metre-high columns give you a sense of past grandeur. A sound and light show takes place in the evening.

activities.text: The Madurai flower market, right beside the Meenakshi Temple, is a festival of colours and fragrances — mountains of jasmine (Madurai's flower), roses and marigolds pile up in baskets. Tamil women wear jasmine garlands in their hair, and the flowers serve as temple offerings. Come early morning when the deliveries arrive.

The Gandhi Memorial Museum, housed in the former Rani Mangammal Palace, traces the Mahatma's life. The most moving exhibit is the bloodstained dhoti Gandhi was wearing when he was assassinated on January 30, 1948. The museum is well curated and free.

Get ready for your Madurai → Rameswaram journey: express trains connect the two cities in 3–4 hours for 170 km. The highlight of the ride is the Pamban Bridge — India's second-longest rail bridge (2.06 km), built in 1914, which crosses the sea on stilts to link Rameswaram Island to the mainland. The train literally runs above the ocean. Grab a window seat.

days_plan.itin_title: India Grand Tour: From the Taj Mahal to the Backwaters
Tamil Nadu and Kerala Road Trip: Temples and Backwaters

visits.text: Mahabalipuram (Mamallapuram) has been a UNESCO site since 1984—it's one of India's oldest rock art ensembles. Begin with the Shore Temple, built in the 8th century by Pallava king Narasimhavarman II. This granite temple facing the sea is one of South India's oldest structural stone temples. Two towers dedicated to Shiva and a Vishnu sanctuary stand facing the Bay of Bengal waves. Arrive at opening time (6am) to enjoy the golden morning light and tranquility—tour buses arrive after 9am.

500 meters south, the Five Rathas (Pancha Rathas) are five monolithic temples each carved from a single granite block in the 7th century. Each bears the name of one of the Pandava brothers from the Mahabharata and illustrates a different architectural style—from simple to complex. It's like an architectural textbook in stone. In front of the largest ratha, a life-size elephant was carved from the same rock.

Don't miss the bas-relief of Arjuna's Penance (or Descent of the Ganges), a sculptural fresco 27 meters long and 9 meters high carved into a rock face. Hundreds of figures—gods, humans, animals, nagas—converge toward a central fissure representing the Ganges descending from heaven. It's one of the world's largest bas-reliefs. Just beside it, Krishna's Butter Ball is an enormous spherical boulder of 250 tons balanced on a slope—no one has ever managed to move it, not even the British with their elephants.

activities.text: Late in the day, head to Mahabalipuram beach. Less touristy than Chennai's beaches, it's lined with fishing catamarans and small restaurants serving grilled fish with your feet in the sand. Walk south toward the new fishing harbor to watch boats returning at dusk.

days_plan.itin_title: South India Circuit: Temples, Backwaters & Wellness

visits.text: The Dharamsala to Manali road is long (about 8 hours) but spectacular - it winds through the Kangra and Kullu valleys, between apple orchards, turquoise rivers, and snowy peaks. Plan stops for the views. Once in Manali, settle in and start with Hadimba Devi Temple, nestled in a cedar forest 2km from town. Built in 1553 by Maharaja Bahadur Singh, it's a four-tiered wooden and stone pagoda temple, dedicated to Hadimba, a demoness from the Mahabharata who became a goddess after marrying warrior Bhima. The architecture is unique in India - the wooden doors are carved with detailed mythological figures.

Climb up to Old Manali, 2km upstream from the modern town. This stone and wood village has kept its Himalayan charm despite the influx of trendy cafés and guesthouses. Traditional wooden and slate houses cling to the slope, and lanes open onto clear views of the peaks. This is where the hippie community settled in the 1970s - the vibe remains laid-back.

Detour to Vashisht, a thermal village 3km from Manali. Natural hot springs feed public pools where locals and travelers bathe side by side. The Vashisht temple, dedicated to the Vedic sage of the same name, is a fine example of Himachali wooden architecture with detailed carvings on the lintels.

Second day in Manali, dedicated to the heights. Head to Solang Valley (14km, 30 min), a natural amphitheater surrounded by snowy peaks. Depending on the season, you'll find paragliding, zorbing, and quad rides (summer), or skiing and sledding (winter). The cable car takes you up to 3,000m altitude with plunging views over the Kullu valley. Arrive early - crowds show up after 10am.

If the pass is open (May to November), push on to Rohtang Pass at 3,980m altitude - the name means 'pile of corpses' in Persian, recalling travelers who died attempting the crossing. The 51km road from Manali crosses landscapes that change dramatically: deodar forests, then alpine meadows, then bare rock and snow. At the summit, the view embraces the Pir Panjal glaciers on one side and Lahaul valley on the other. Bring warm clothes even in summer. A permit is mandatory - request it online the day before.

Alternative if Rohtang is closed: take the Atal Tunnel, the world's longest high-altitude road tunnel (9.02km), inaugurated in 2020. It connects Kullu valley to Lahaul valley in 30 minutes instead of 4 to 6 hours via the pass. On the other side, Sissu village and its waterfall offer a first glimpse of desert Lahaul - a striking contrast with Manali's greenery.

activities.text: Walk along the Beas River, which flows through the Kullu valley. In season (May to July, September to October), operators offer whitewater rafting between Pirdi and Jhiri - a 14km stretch with class II to III rapids, perfect for a first experience.

Taste Himachali specialties: siddu (steamed bread stuffed with nuts and spices), madra (chickpeas in yogurt sauce), and fresh trout caught from the Beas, grilled or curried. Old Manali restaurants have the best menus.

Late in the day, climb to Manu Temple, dedicated to sage Manu, considered in Hindu mythology as humanity's creator - Manali takes its name from him. The temple is modest but the path offers clear valley views.

Try tandem paragliding from Solang Valley or Marhi - Himalayan thermals carry you above the valley for 15 to 20 minutes. The sensation of soaring facing the 6,000-meter massif is unforgettable. Operators offer the flight with GoPro video included.

Stop at Vashisht hot springs on the way back if you didn't visit them yesterday. After a day at altitude, a bath in naturally hot sulfurous water is the best remedy for fatigue. Locals come at dawn - late afternoon you'll have more space.

visits.text: Less known than Tadoba or Pench, Kawal Tiger Reserve is nonetheless Telangana's largest nature reserve, declared a tiger reserve in 2012. It plays a corridor role between central India's forests and the Eastern Ghats. The reception center is at Jannaram, 50 km from Mancherial. Leopards, gaurs, wolves, and around 300 bird species have been recorded.

activities.text: You take a brief morning safari from Jannaram Gate, covering 15 km of forest track with watchtowers included in the circuit. Official price around 3,500 rupees per vehicle. Booking through the Forest Department; the park closes July to September for monsoon.

days_plan.itin_title: From Coast to Jungle: Bhubaneswar to Bhopal in 16 Days

visits.text: Cross the 450 acres of Vijay Vilas Palace estate, built between 1920 and 1929 for the son of the Maharao of Kutch. The chhatris, jharokas, and red sandstone jalis evoke Rajasthan palaces, but the sea breeze reminds you the Arabian Sea lies meters away. The royal family still resides in the upper floors. The private beach below—2 km—opens to visitors.

The carpenters at Mandvi's port call it 'making a boat the old way': solid-wood dhows still emerge from Mandvi's shipyard, built without plans, by eye and mallet. This yard is one of the last active in Gujarat for traditional wooden boat construction destined for trade in the Persian Gulf. Stop by before 1pm when activity peaks.

activities.text: If time allows, Mandvi Beach after the palace visit: a golden sand beach with few visitors, without the tourist infrastructure of classic coastal resorts. Sunset over the Arabian Sea from this beach is quieter than elsewhere on the coast. Avoid monsoon when waves are dangerous.

days_plan.itin_title: Gujarat: Grand UNESCO Sites Tour in 15 Days

visits.text: Medieval astronomers designed Modhera's Sun Temple so that at both equinoxes, the first rays of sun pass straight through the sanctuary without casting a shadow. Built around 1026-27 by King Bhima I of the Solanki dynasty, the temple has been decommissioned since the invasions—no ritual is celebrated here—but this absence preserves a rare formal purity. Arrive at opening at 8am for raking light on the friezes.

The Surya Kund, a rectangular tank 123 feet long according to archaeological surveys, precedes the temple. Its 108 small shrines descend in tiers, mirrored in the water—the symmetry is total, the silence too. The tank was used for ritual ablutions before the temple was withdrawn from worship. Don't miss the adjacent museum, open 10am–5pm except Friday.

activities.text: Stay for sunset: the sound-and-light show at Modhera's Sun Temple projects the history of the Solankis onto the carved walls. Artificial light reveals details invisible in daylight—particularly the erotic frieze on the basement, often overlooked. Evening sessions, separate fee from entry (admission: ₹40 Indians / ₹600 foreigners in 2026).

days_plan.itin_title: Gujarat: Grand UNESCO Sites Tour in 15 Days

visits.text: The drive from Alleppey to Munnar takes 4 to 5 hours, but it's magnificent—you pass from flat paddy fields to the first hills of the Western Ghats, with waterfalls along the way (stop at Cheeyappara if you have time). Munnar sits at 1,600 m altitude, and tea plantations stretch as far as the eye can see across the hills. The first plants were installed here by Scottish planters in the late 19th century. Wander through the rows of tea bushes—the Kannan Devan estate alone covers 24,000 hectares. For a real immersion, ask to observe the morning picking (7am-9am), when women in saris move through the fields with their large baskets.

Step into the Munnar Tea Museum (Kannan Devan Tea Museum), 3.5 km from town center. Inaugurated in 2005 by the Tata Group, it traces the history of the plantations from the British pioneers. You'll see a manual tea roller from 1905, a 1920s Pelton wheel that powered the factories, and a locomotive wheel from the railway that ran between Munnar and Top Station. The museum offers two demonstration sessions per day (10am and 2pm) and a paid tasting of different teas. Closed Mondays. Allow 45 minutes to an hour.

Second day in Munnar: head to Mattupetty Dam, 13 km from town center. This artificial lake surrounded by tea-covered hills and shola forests is one of the region's most beautiful spots. You can take a motorboat tour on the lake, or simply walk along the shore to enjoy the calm. On the way, stop at Echo Point—shout toward the hills and your voice bounces back clearly. It's touristy, but kids love it.

Continue to Kundala Lake, 20 km from Munnar. This high-altitude lake is surrounded by tea plantations and forests. Pedal boats and shikaras (small traditional boats from Kashmir) are available for a leisurely ride. The road there winds through postcard-perfect landscapes—keep your camera ready.

activities.text: Wake before dawn to watch sunrise from Munnar's hills. The most spectacular viewpoints are Pothamedu (5 km from town center) and Top Station (32 km away, Munnar's highest point at 1,880 m). When the mist lifts from the green undulations of the plantations, the spectacle is breathtaking. Bring a sweater—at this altitude, mornings are cool, often around 10°C.

If you have energy to spare, hike in Eravikulam National Park, 15 km from Munnar. This 97 km² park in the Western Ghats is home to the endangered Nilgiri tahr, a wild goat that's surprisingly approachable here. The main trail runs about 2 km round-trip across grassy slopes. Every twelve years, these hills blanket themselves in blue neelakurinji flowers—the next bloom is expected in 2030.

Between sites, make a stop at Chinnakanal Waterfalls (also called Power House Falls), 18 km from Munnar. Water tumbles from 2,000 m altitude through dense vegetation—a particularly impressive sight after the monsoon. The path to get closer is short but slippery, so wear good shoes.

End the day with a jeep tour of a tea plantation. The Lockhart estate, accessible only by 4x4, offers an experience away from the crowds: you drive between rows of tea bushes, watch the manufacturing process in the mini-factory, and taste freshly brewed tea. Book in advance through your accommodation—spaces are limited.

visits.text: Munroe Island is an archipelago of eight islets at the confluence of Ashtamudi Lake and the Kallada River, 25 km from Kollam. Here, no crowded houseboats like in Alleppey—you glide in a dugout canoe, maneuvered with a bamboo pole, through channels so narrow that palm fronds brush your shoulders. The island takes its name from Colonel John Munro, a British administrator of Travancore in the 19th century. Book a morning paddle of 2 to 3 hours: you'll pass through natural mangrove arches, drift past houses on stilts, and cross paths with fishermen casting their nets. This is the peaceful, authentic alternative to the touristy backwaters.

After the canoe ride, explore the village by bicycle—most homestays lend bikes free of charge. The 5 km loop around the island is flat and shaded by coconut palms. You'll traverse coconut plantations, paddy fields, and coir-making workshops. Stop at Edachal bridge to watch fishermen at work, and if you come across a coir workshop, ask for a demonstration—artisans have been hand-braiding the fiber for generations.

activities.text: Late in the day, join a Kerala cooking class at a local home. Your host will show you how to prepare a traditional fish curry with coconut milk, appam (fermented rice pancakes), and aviyal, a mixed vegetable dish typical of Kerala. You'll eat everything served on a banana leaf, sitting on the floor in local style. It's also a chance to taste toddy, a mildly alcoholic drink tapped from coconut palm sap—the local specialty.

days_plan.itin_title: South India Circuit: Temples, Backwaters & Wellness

visits.text: The Maharaja's Palace of Mysore is one of India's most visited monuments after the Taj Mahal. Built between 1897 and 1912 by British architect Henry Irwin, it blends Indo-Saracenic, Gothic, and Hindu styles. The durbar (audience) halls are covered in gold leaf, stained glass, carved columns, and solid silver doors. The golden throne, made of fig wood plated with 280 kg of gold, is brought out only during the Dasara festival in October. Allow 1.5 hours for the visit. Important: you must remove your shoes, and cameras are prohibited inside.

After the palace, dive into Devaraja Market, a ten-minute walk away. This covered bazaar, one of Mysore's oldest, overflows with pyramids of flowers (jasmine, roses, marigold), spices, tropical fruits, and silks. Don't leave without trying the crispy dosas from the stalls—Mysore masala dosas, stuffed with spiced potatoes and green chutney, are a local specialty. If you have a sweet tooth, Mysore pak (a ghee and chickpea flour cake) is a classic.

activities.text: On Sundays and public holidays, as well as during Dasara, the palace lights up with 97,000 bulbs at 7pm. The spectacle lasts about an hour and transforms the building into a golden jewel against the night. Even on weekdays, stroll through the illuminated gardens. Arrive 30 minutes early to secure a good spot—crowds can be dense.

If you have time in the afternoon, climb Chamundi Hill (1,065 m), 13 km from town center. You can drive up or climb the 1,000 steps. Along the way, you'll pass the monolithic Nandi, 5 meters high and carved from a single block of black granite. At the summit, Chamundeshwari Temple offers a panorama over the entire city and surrounding plains.

days_plan.itin_title: South India Circuit: Temples, Backwaters & Wellness

visits.text: Nagarjunasagar Dam, built on the Krishna between 1955 and 1969, is one of the world's largest masonry dams. It bears the name of Acharya Nagarjuna, founder of Mahayana Buddhism, who taught in this valley. The view from the dam crest over the reservoir is vertiginous.

Nagarjunakonda island is 90% underwater: what remains accessible at the summit is actually the former hilltop where archaeologists relocated stupas, viharas, and Buddhist sculptures from the 1st-3rd centuries CE before flooding. Acharya Nagarjuna himself is said to have taught here, drawing students from Sri Lanka and China. Closed on Fridays.

activities.text: Take the ferry at 9:30 a.m. or 11:30 a.m. from Vijayapuri to reach Nagarjunakonda island: the crossing lasts about 20 minutes across the reservoir. Last return 5:30 p.m. Bring water and a hat: the island is exposed, with no notable shade outside the museum.

11 km from Nagarjunasagar, Ethipothala Falls plunge 21 meters at the confluence of three rivers. A viewing platform overlooks the basin. Less known than the dam, this stop adds 30 minutes to the itinerary and suits children well.

days_plan.itin_title: Deccan Trail: Bahmani Sultans and Kakatiya Temples in 12 Days

visits.text: On October 14, 1956, Ambedkar and hundreds of thousands of his supporters embraced Buddhism in Nagpur, in this space now known as Deekshabhoomi. The great white marble stupa—22 meters high, 36 in diameter—contains the ashes of Dr. Ambedkar, architect of the Indian Constitution. One million visitors come annually during Dhammachakra Pravartan Din, the anniversary of the conversion. Accessible to all.

Futala Lake comes alive in late afternoon: joggers, families, bhel puri vendors, and sugarcane juice. It's Nagpur's most frequented urban park. Musical fountain show some evenings. Free entry. Best time: sunset.

activities.text: You finish at Sitabuldi Market, ideal for Nagpur oranges—the region is one of India's major producers. In season (November to January), stalls overflow. Also look for local chiwda and peda. Allow an hour to browse.

days_plan.itin_title: From Coast to Jungle: Bhubaneswar to Bhopal in 16 Days

visits.text: The road from Aurangabad to Nashik (225 km, 4.5 hours) crosses the Deccan plateau before descending toward the verdant hills of the Nashik valley. Nashik is one of Hinduism's holiest cities—it hosts the Kumbh Mela every 12 years, the world's largest human gathering (tens of millions of pilgrims). The Godavari River, which Hindus consider the "Ganges of the South," flows through the city.

Explore the sacred ghats along the Godavari—Ramkund is the most important, the place where according to tradition Rama bathed during his exile. Pilgrims bathe here to wash away their sins, and this is where funerary ash immersion rites take place. The atmosphere is intense: temple bells, chants, marigold flowers floating on the water, priests in white dhotis.

If time permits, push on to Trimbakeshwar, 30 km from Nashik—one of the 12 Jyotirlinga (Shiva's most sacred temples in India). The current black stone temple, built in the 18th century by Peshwa Balaji Baji Rao, is surrounded by green hills. It's also the source of the Godavari, which rises on Brahmagiri hill just above. The sanctuary interior is reserved for Hindus, but the exterior architecture and atmosphere are worth the visit.

For archaeology enthusiasts, Pandavleni Caves (or Trirashmi) are a complex of 24 Buddhist caves carved between the 3rd century BC and 2nd century AD—among Maharashtra's oldest. Less spectacular than Ajanta, they offer a glimpse of primitive Buddhist monastic architecture in a peaceful setting.

activities.text: Surprise: Nashik is India's "wine capital." Its altitude (around 600 m), dry climate, and volcanic soils create conditions close to California's Napa Valley. Several wineries are open to visitors—Sula Vineyards, the pioneer, offers tastings with views over the lake and vines. York Winery and Grover Zampa are other good options. End the day with a glass of local Chenin Blanc watching the sunset over the hills—an unexpected contrast with the morning's millennia-old temples.

days_plan.itin_title: Mumbai & Maharashtra Circuit: Sacred Caves and the Konkan Coast

visits.text: Start with the Red Fort, a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2007 — its red sandstone walls rise 23 metres high and encircle a 100-hectare complex. Shah Jahan had it built between 1638 and 1648 as the imperial palace of his new capital, Shahjahanabad. Don't miss the Diwan-i-Khas, the Hall of Private Audiences, whose ceiling was once studded with precious stones. Every August 15th, India's Prime Minister hoists the national flag here — you'll spot the podium from the ramparts. Allow at least 1.5 hours, and arrive at opening time (9:30 AM) to beat the crowds.

Jama Masjid is a five-minute walk from the Red Fort — it's the largest mosque in India, built by Shah Jahan between 1644 and 1656. Its inner courtyard can hold 25,000 worshippers. Climb to the top of the south minaret (around 40 metres) for a 360° panorama over Old Delhi — you'll see the tangled rooftops of Chandni Chowk and, on a clear day, the Parliament dome in the distance. Remove your shoes at the entrance, and if you're wearing shorts, you'll be lent a sarong.

Dive into Chandni Chowk, Delhi's oldest commercial artery — Shah Jahan laid it out in 1650. It's a labyrinth of alleyways where spices, fabrics, jewellery and electronics jostle for space. Make your way to Paranthe Wali Gali, a narrow lane famous for its paranthas fried in ghee — stalls have been serving them here since the 1870s. Fair warning: the crowds are intense, so keep your belongings close.

Make a detour to Raj Ghat, Gandhi's memorial — a slab of black marble marks the spot where he was cremated on January 31, 1948, one day after his assassination. The site is simple and deeply moving, surrounded by lawns. An eternal flame burns here at all times. Allow 30 minutes, barefoot on the marble.

Head to New Delhi this morning — start with Humayun's Tomb, a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1993. Built in 1570, it's the first great Mughal garden-tomb and the direct blueprint for the Taj Mahal, built 60 years later. The white marble dome stands 42 metres tall, surrounded by symmetrical gardens divided into squares (the Persian charbagh layout). Arrive early in the morning — the golden light on the red sandstone is stunning, and you'll have the place almost to yourself before 10 AM.

You can't miss the Qutub Minar — this 72.5-metre brick tower is the tallest minaret in the world made from this material. Construction began in 1192 under Qutb ud-Din Aibak, Delhi's first sultan, and was completed by his successor Iltutmish. Look closely at the Arabic inscriptions that cover the tower from base to top — each floor features a different balcony style. You can no longer climb to the top (closed after an accident in 1981), but the base is impressive. Right next to it, stop by the 4th-century Iron Pillar that has resisted rust for 1,600 years — a metallurgical mystery no one has cracked yet.

India Gate, Delhi's triumphal arch, stands 42 metres tall. It honours the 70,000 Indian soldiers who died during World War I. In the evening, the monument is lit up and the esplanade becomes a promenade for local families. It's a good spot for a midday break.

Finish at Lodi Gardens, a 36-hectare park that houses 15th-century tombs from the Sayyid and Lodi dynasties — octagonal buildings set amid immaculate lawns. Delhiites come here to jog or picnic. If you have time, seek out the Tomb of Mohammed Shah (1450) and the Bara Gumbad Mosque.

Back in Delhi — full circle. If you arrive on the morning Vande Bharat Express, you have the afternoon free. Visit what you missed on Days 1–2: the Akshardham Temple (2005) is a spectacular modern complex covering 40 hectares on the banks of the Yamuna — 20,000 statues carved in pink sandstone and white marble. Admission is free but security checks are strict (no phones or cameras allowed inside).

Hauz Khas Village, in south Delhi, centres on a 14th-century reservoir (built by Alauddin Khalji) surrounded by medieval ruins — now reimagined as a trendy quarter with art galleries, designer boutiques and rooftop restaurants. The ruins of the madrasa and the tomb of Firoz Shah Tughlaq overlook the lake — a peaceful and photogenic spot.

activities.text: Take a cycle-rickshaw ride through the alleyways of Old Delhi — it's the best way to weave between market stalls and sacred cows. Negotiate the fare before hopping on (expect around 30–45 minutes for a full tour of the neighbourhood). You'll pass the Gurudwara Sis Ganj Sahib, a 17th-century Sikh temple you can enter freely — and even enjoy a free meal at the langar, the communal kitchen.

In the evening, if you still have energy, catch the Red Fort's sound and light show — the 45-minute spectacle traces Mughal history with the illuminated walls as a backdrop. The English show starts around 8:30 PM (check times on-site, as they change with the season).

In the late afternoon, walk to the Dargah of Hazrat Nizamuddin — Delhi's most revered Sufi shrine, founded in the 14th century. Every Thursday evening, musicians perform qawwali in the courtyard — the atmosphere is electric, somewhere between devotion and trance. Arrive around 6 PM to settle in before the crowd thickens.

Get ready for your departure to Agra tomorrow morning: the Gatiman Express leaves Hazrat Nizamuddin station at 8:10 AM and arrives at Agra Cantonment at 9:50 AM — that's 100 minutes for 188 km, making it one of India's fastest trains. Book your tickets on IRCTC at least 3–4 days ahead. AC Chair Car is recommended. Note: this train does not run on Fridays.

Last-minute shopping at Khan Market or Connaught Place — for souvenirs, spices, tea, textiles. Khan Market is Delhi's most upscale shopping area, with excellent bookshops and cafés. Connaught Place (CP) is a circle of white colonnades built in 1933 — more down-to-earth, livelier, with the metro right below.

Farewell dinner — Delhi is the gastronomic capital of North India. Butter chicken (invented here in the 1950s), chole bhature, Old Delhi kebabs, Nizamuddin biryani. Thirty days of travel, thousands of kilometres of track, and India stays unpredictable, intense and overwhelming right through to the final meal.
